title: Transcranial direct current stimulation over the left DLPFC for major depressive disorder: a double-blind randomised controlled trial
abstract: Background: Transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS) has emerged as a promising non-invasive neuromodulation approach for major depressive disorder (MDD). We conducted a double-blind, sham-controlled randomised trial of anodal tDCS over the left dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC) in adults with MDD. Methods: Forty adults meeting DSM-5 criteria for MDD (HAM-D ≥ 18) were randomised 1:1 to active tDCS (2 mA, F3 anode, Fp2 cathode, 20 min/session, 20 sessions over 4 weeks) or sham. The primary outcome was HAM-D reduction at week 4. Results: Active tDCS produced a significantly greater reduction in HAM-D scores compared with sham (mean difference 6.8 points, Cohen d=0.72, p<0.001). Response rates were 60% (active) versus 25% (sham). Remission rates were 35% (active) versus 10% (sham). No serious adverse events were recorded. Conclusions: tDCS targeting the left DLPFC is effective and safe in adults with MDD, supporting its use as a stand-alone or adjunctive treatment.
s2_tldr: Active tDCS over left DLPFC significantly reduced HAM-D scores versus sham in adults with MDD over 20 sessions.

title: Repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation for major depressive disorder: a comprehensive meta-analysis of 78 randomised controlled trials
abstract: Objective: To synthesise evidence on the efficacy of repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) for major depressive disorder (MDD) from randomised controlled trials published between 2000 and 2021. Methods: We searched MEDLINE, EMBASE, and PsycINFO for RCTs comparing active rTMS with sham in adults with MDD. Seventy-eight trials (n=5,241 patients) met inclusion criteria. Data were pooled using random-effects meta-analysis. Primary outcome was standardised mean difference in depression rating scale scores. Results: Active rTMS produced a large, statistically significant antidepressant effect (SMD=-0.87, 95% CI [-1.02, -0.71], p<0.0001). High-frequency rTMS over the left DLPFC showed the largest effect sizes. iTBS demonstrated non-inferior efficacy to high-frequency rTMS in a subset of trials. Heterogeneity was moderate (I²=54%). Publication bias was minimal. Conclusions: rTMS is an effective antidepressant with a moderate-to-large effect size across diverse MDD populations.

title: Photobiomodulation for chronic insomnia: an open-label pilot study
abstract: This open-label pilot study examined the feasibility and preliminary efficacy of transcranial photobiomodulation (tPBM) in ten adults with chronic insomnia disorder (ISI ≥ 15). Participants received 12 sessions of near-infrared light (810 nm, 250 mW/cm², 20 min) delivered to frontal cortex over four weeks. The Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI) improved from a mean of 14.3 to 8.1 at week 4 (p=0.03). Actigraphy-confirmed sleep onset latency decreased by 18 minutes on average. No adverse events were reported.

title: High-frequency rTMS over the right DLPFC reduces anxiety severity in generalised anxiety disorder: a sham-controlled pilot
abstract: We investigated whether high-frequency repetitive TMS (rTMS; 20 Hz, 110% RMT, 1600 pulses/session) applied to the right dorsolateral prefrontal cortex could reduce anxiety in adults with generalised anxiety disorder (GAD, HAM-A ≥ 18). Fifteen patients completed 15 sessions over three weeks in an active or sham-controlled design. The Hamilton Anxiety Rating Scale (HAM-A) decreased by a mean of 9.2 points in the active group versus 2.1 points in the sham group (p=0.02). Remission was achieved in 33% of active patients. These preliminary results support a larger definitive RCT of right DLPFC rTMS for GAD.
s2_tldr: High-frequency rTMS over right DLPFC reduced HAM-A scores in GAD patients compared to sham.

title: Transcranial direct current stimulation over the left DLPFC for major depressive disorder: a double-blind randomised controlled trial
abstract: Background: Transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS) has emerged as a promising non-invasive neuromodulation approach for major depressive disorder (MDD). We conducted a double-blind, sham-controlled randomised trial of anodal tDCS over the left dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC) in adults with MDD. Methods: Forty adults meeting DSM-5 criteria for MDD (HAM-D ≥ 18) were randomised 1:1 to active tDCS (2 mA, F3 anode, Fp2 cathode, 20 min/session, 20 sessions over 4 weeks) or sham. The primary outcome was HAM-D reduction at week 4. Results: Active tDCS produced a significantly greater reduction in HAM-D scores compared with sham (mean difference 6.8 points, Cohen d=0.72, p<0.001). Response rates were 60% (active) versus 25% (sham). Remission rates were 35% (active) versus 10% (sham). No serious adverse events were recorded. Conclusions: tDCS targeting the left DLPFC is effective and safe in adults with MDD, supporting its use as a stand-alone or adjunctive treatment.
s2_tldr: Active tDCS over left DLPFC significantly reduced HAM-D scores versus sham in adults with MDD over 20 sessions.

title: Astrocyte calcium signalling gates synaptic plasticity via tripartite synapse modulation in rodent hippocampus
abstract: Astrocytes actively participate in synaptic transmission through the release of gliotransmitters, forming the so-called tripartite synapse. Here we demonstrate that astrocyte-mediated calcium signalling gates long-term potentiation (LTP) in the rodent hippocampus. Using two-photon calcium imaging combined with optogenetic silencing of astrocytes, we show that suppression of astrocyte calcium transients abolishes LTP induction at Schaffer collateral-CA1 synapses. Pharmacological block of IP3-mediated calcium release in astrocytes mimicked this effect. These findings reveal a glial gate for synaptic plasticity with implications for understanding memory consolidation at the cellular level. No clinical intervention or human subjects were involved in this study.
s2_tldr: Astrocyte calcium signalling gates LTP in rodent hippocampus via tripartite synapse mechanisms.
